overview.body: Welcome to Bat City Collective! You've just joined Austin's hottest indie game studio as a junior developer. Your first project? Building a Texas road trip game for our client, All My Eggses Live in Texas. In this assignment, you'll create the travel calculator — getting the player's name, simulating a dice roll, and calculating how far they can travel toward each destination.

mentorQuote.quote: Hey, welcome aboard! This first assignment is all about getting comfortable with Python basics — variables, input, and calculations. No decisions yet. You'll get a roll, calculate miles, and see how far that gets you toward each destination. Think of it as rolling the dice before you decide where to go!

objectives: Display a welcome banner for the game
Get a dice roll (1–20) and convert it to an integer
Calculate remaining miles to each of 4 destinations
Display a Travel Report showing all calculations
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 2: Input, Processing, and Output

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_CORPUS
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Corpus Christi (150 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_HOUSTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Houston (200 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_AUSTIN
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Austin (80 miles)

namedConstants.name: HOU_TO_GALVESTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from Houston to Galveston (50 miles)

programRequirements: Your program MUST define 4 named constants for distances
Your program MUST use int(input()) to get the dice roll
Your program MUST calculate miles_rolled based on your dice roll (each pip = 10 miles)
Your program MUST calculate remaining miles to each destination
Your program MUST include your docstring with name, abc123, section, and description
overview.saveYourCode: Keep lab-1-1.py — Module 1.2 adds decision structures directly on top of your travel calculator.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. Everything here is new — you're building from scratch.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----  [NEW]
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80
HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----  [NEW]
def main():
│
│── Display welcome banner               [NEW-CP1]
│── Get player_name from input()          [NEW-CP2]
│── Display welcome message with f-string [NEW-CP2]
│── Get roll from int(input())            [NEW-CP2]
│
│── miles_rolled = roll * 10             [NEW-CP3]
│── Calculate remaining to each dest     [NEW-CP3]
│
│── Display TRAVEL REPORT                [NEW-CP4]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: The foundation: a Python file with proper documentation, 4 distance constants, and a game banner.
Named constants make your code readable and maintainable. If a distance changes, you update ONE line instead of hunting through your entire program.

items: Create a new file called lab-1-1.py
Add your docstring at the very top with your name, abc123, section, and a description
Define 4 constants for the distances from San Antonio (see the Named Constants section below)
Add the main() function structure below your constants. All programs in this course use this pattern — copy it exactly (you'll learn why in Chapter 5)
Inside main(), create a welcome banner using print() and string multiplication (notice the 4-space indent)

content.code:
# Constants - distances from San Antonio (in miles)
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80

# Distance from Houston to Galveston
HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50

content.title: Important: Indentation
content.body: From now on, ALL your code (except constants and imports) goes inside the main() function, indented 4 spaces. The if __name__ block always stays at the very bottom of your file.

content.code:
# Welcome banner
    print()
    print('=' * 50)
    print('DEEP IN THE HEART: A LONE STAR JOURNEY')
    print('=' * 50)
    print()

references.description: Shows how '=' * 50 creates a string of 50 equal signs. Perfect for banners! to *: '=' * 50 creates a string of 50 equal signs. Perfect for banners!

rows: NameError: Python thinks it's a variable name
NameError when you try to use it later
Style violation — constants should be ALL_CAPS

items: Docstring with your name, abc123, section, and description
4 named constants: SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, SAT_TO_AUSTIN, HOU_TO_GALVESTON
The main() function structure with the if __name__ block at the bottom
A welcome banner (inside main) that displays when you run the program

content.body: Two input prompts: one for the player's name (string), and one for their dice roll (integer 1–20).

references.description: Shows the pattern age = int(input('What is your age? ')) — this is EXACTLY what you need for the dice roll!

content.quote: The key insight here is that input() always returns a string. When you need to do math with the user's input, you have to convert it to a number first using int(). This is Program 2-13 in action!

items: Get the player's name using input() and store it in a variable
Display a welcome message using an f-string
Display instructions for the dice roll (point them to the random number generator)
Get the roll using int(input()) and store it in a variable

references.description: Getting string and integer input — use the same pattern with your own prompts
Using f-strings to embed variables in output — same pattern with your variable names

content.title: Required Prompts
content.body: CodeGrade checks for exact prompt text. Use these strings:
items: Player name: 'What is your name, traveler? '
Dice roll: 'What did you roll? (1-20): '

rows: NameError: Python thinks prompt is a variable
Program doesn't pause for user input

items: Variable player_name storing the player's name (string)
Variable roll storing the dice roll (integer)
Welcome message with player's name displayed

content.body: The math engine! Convert the roll to miles, then subtract from each destination distance to see how far you'd still need to travel.

references.description: Shows the pattern of storing calculation results: pay = salary + bonus. You'll do similar math here.

items: Calculate miles from the roll: multiply the roll by 10 and store in miles_rolled
Calculate remaining miles to Corpus: subtract miles_rolled from SAT_TO_CORPUS
Calculate remaining miles to Houston: same pattern, different constant
Calculate remaining miles to Austin: same pattern, different constant
Calculate Galveston total first: add SAT_TO_HOUSTON + HOU_TO_GALVESTON
Calculate remaining to Galveston: subtract miles_rolled from the total

content.body: Step 1: Add debug lines AFTER your calculations:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE AFTER TESTING
    print(f'DEBUG: miles_rolled = {miles_rolled}')
    print(f'DEBUG: corpus_remaining = {corpus_remaining}')
    print(f'DEBUG: galveston_total = {galveston_total}')

rows: Make sure you used int(input()), not just input()
Negative means you made it with miles to spare!

items: Variable miles_rolled calculated from your dice roll
Variables for remaining miles to each destination
Variable galveston_total = 250

content.body: The final output! A nicely formatted Travel Report showing the roll, miles, and remaining distance to each destination.

references.description: Shows how to embed variables in strings: f'Your name is {name}'

items: Display the Travel Report header with banner lines
Show the roll results: driver name, roll, and miles rolled
Display each destination with its distance and remaining miles after the roll
Add helpful text explaining what negative/positive means

content.output:
==================================================
DEEP IN THE HEART: A LONE STAR JOURNEY
==================================================

What is your name, traveler? Maria

Welcome, Maria!
You are in San Antonio. Your goal: reach the beach!

Roll to see how far you can travel.
Go to this link and click Generate:
https://www.calculator.net/random-number-generator.html

What did you roll? (1-20): 15

==================================================
TRAVEL REPORT
==================================================
Driver: Maria
You rolled: 15
Miles you can travel: 150
==================================================

DISTANCES FROM SAN ANTONIO:

  Corpus Christi: 150 miles (BEACH!)
    After your roll: 0 miles remaining

  Houston: 200 miles
    After your roll: 50 miles remaining

  Austin: 80 miles
    After your roll: -70 miles remaining

  Galveston (via Houston): 250 miles
    After your roll: 100 miles remaining

Negative = you made it with miles to spare!
Positive = you still have miles to go.

rows: Variables show as literal text like {player_name}
Output looks cramped and hard to read

items: A complete Travel Report with all 4 destinations
Each destination shows total distance and remaining after roll
Helpful explanation of negative vs. positive remaining

finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 2 ONLY: variables and assignment, input() with optional int() or float() conversion, print() statements, arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /), f-strings for formatting, string multiplication ('=' * 50), and named constants (ALL_CAPS). NOT permitted: if statements, loops, lists, or any Chapter 3+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template you copy exactly — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.

specific: input() with optional int() or float() conversion
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: if statements, loops, lists, or any Chapter 3+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template you copy exactly — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.
